metadata.title: Build a Spaced Study Scheduler with Claude: Memory Engineering Before the Exam

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The comparison that hit me was 6 retrieval events versus 1, and 4 sleep-consolidation windows versus 0, for the exact same seven hours. I want to see how the advantage decays as the window shrinks. Help me: (1) rerun exam_scheduler.py for a 3-day window instead of 7 with the same five topics, (2) count how many retrieval events and sleep windows survive the compression while still keeping each topic in front of me at least twice, (3) name the point where spacing collapses into cramming.

props.segment: Build a Spaced Study Scheduler with

beats.narration_text: Same 7 hours, different schedule: 71 vs. 89. The schedule is the lever.

Spacing and retrieval practice are the two highest-utility study techniques in the research literature. Both require advance planning — you can't space a session you're starting tonight. The scheduler does the planning: given an exam date, a topic list, and available hours, it generates the schedule that puts each topic in front of you at least twice across multiple sessions with sleep-consolidation windows between them.
Ask Claude to write exam_scheduler.py with spaced interleaved sessions and retrieval activity rotation.

props.command: claude "Write exam_scheduler.py that takes: exam_date (YYYY-MM-DD), topics (list of strings), hours_per_day (dict: day → max hours). Generate a spaced interleaved study schedule: each topic appears at least twice, final day before exam is practice test, no two sessions same calendar day. For each session, assign a retrieval activity (close-and-recall, practice problems, teach-back prompt, or mixed test). Print as markdown calendar and save to study_schedule.md."

beats.narration_text: The session-generation logic: topic cycling for interleaving, minimum-twice enforcement, practice-test designation for final session, retrieval-activity rotation.

props.code:
ACTIVITIES = ["close-and-recall","practice problems",
              "teach-back","mixed test"]

def build_schedule(exam_date, topics, hours_per_day):
    sessions = []
    topic_count = {t: 0 for t in topics}
    activity_idx = 0
    days = sorted(hours_per_day.keys())
    topic_cycle = itertools.cycle(topics)
    for day in days[:-1]:          # reserve last day for practice test
        hrs = hours_per_day[day]
        n = max(1, int(hrs / 1.5)) # sessions per day
        for _ in range(n):
            t = next(topic_cycle)
            act = ACTIVITIES[activity_idx % len(ACTIVITIES)]
            sessions.append((day, t, act))
            topic_count[t] += 1
            activity_idx += 1
    # Ensure every topic appears at least twice
    for t, cnt in topic_count.items():
        if cnt < 2:
            sessions.append((days[-2], t, "mixed test"))
    sessions.append((days[-1], "ALL TOPICS", "PRACTICE TEST"))
    return sessions

beats.narration_text: Demo run: 7-day window, 5 topics — Chapters 7 through 11, 1.5 hours per day. Schedule generated: Day 1 Ch7 plus Ch9 close-and-recall, Day 3 Ch8 plus Ch10 practice problems, Day 4 Ch11 plus Ch7 teach-back, Day 5 Ch8 plus Ch9 mixed test, Day 6 Ch10 plus Ch11 practice problems second pass, Day 7 PRACTICE TEST all topics. Each topic appears exactly twice before the practice test. Four sleep-consolidation windows between sessions. study_schedule.md saved.
Add --cramming-mode flag and show worst-case comparison.

props.command: python3 exam_scheduler.py --exam-date 2026-07-20 --topics Ch7 Ch8 Ch9 Ch10 Ch11 --hours 1.5 --cramming-mode

beats.narration_text: Cramming mode output: all 7 hours on Day 6, the night before. Retrieval opportunities: 1, versus spacing's 6. Sleep-consolidation windows: 0, versus spacing's 4. Comparison printed: Cramming schedule: 1 retrieval event, 0 sleep-consolidation windows. Spacing schedule: 6 retrieval events, 4 sleep-consolidation windows. Research prediction: 12 to 18 point difference on next-day recall. The --cramming-mode flag is the lesson — it makes the cost of the default visible.

The scheduler does not do the studying — it does the planning. The planning costs 5 minutes. The payoff is 4 additional retrieval exercises and 4 sleep-consolidation windows per exam, compared to cramming. The research on this is 50 years old. What changes is running the schedule before the night before.
Next: build a budget stress-tester that shows the cascade before the money moves.
